clean_output:
summary: Repository-level context files did not reliably help coding agents in these evaluations, and they usually raised cost even when developer-written files performed better than LLM-generated ones.
claims:
  - claim_id: claim-001
    claim_text: Across the reported evaluations, supplying repository-level context files tended to lower coding-agent task success relative to giving no repository context.
    claim_kind: empirical_claim
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-01
    mechanism_or_bounds: The evidence establishes the direction of the aggregate performance effect, but it does not justify a single causal mechanism for why context files hurt success across all agents and tasks.
    contradiction_note: This overall result is in visible tension with the subset result that developer-provided context files improved performance relative to omitting context files.
    confidence: medium
  - claim_id: claim-002
    claim_text: Developer-provided context files performed better than LLM-generated context files on average, improving performance by 4% relative to no context while LLM-generated files reduced performance by 3%.
    claim_kind: comparative_claim
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-03
      - evidence-04
      - evidence-10
    mechanism_or_bounds: The comparison is supported across evaluated agents, but the evidence here does not isolate which properties of developer-written files caused the advantage.
    contradiction_note: This difference does not erase the broader negative aggregate finding, so the evidence supports a qualified comparison rather than a general claim that context files are beneficial.
    confidence: medium
  - claim_id: claim-003
    claim_text: Repository-level context files raised inference cost substantially, with reported average increases above 20%, and LLM-generated files also increased agent steps and reasoning-token use.
    claim_kind: bounded_claim
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-02
      - evidence-09
      - evidence-18
    mechanism_or_bounds: The most direct bound is that added context was associated with more agent activity and token use, but the evidence does not fully separate whether the extra cost came from useful exploration, redundant rereading, or both.
    contradiction_note: No direct contradiction is visible on the cost increase; the main tension is that higher cost did not consistently buy higher success.
    confidence: high
  - claim_id: claim-004
    claim_text: The evidence supports a bounded mechanism that context files change agent behavior toward more repository-specific exploration and tool use, but this extra activity did not clearly help agents reach the relevant modified files sooner.
    claim_kind: mechanism_claim
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-13
      - evidence-14
      - evidence-16
      - evidence-17
    mechanism_or_bounds: Context files appear to steer agents into additional searching, reading, testing, and tool invocation, including strong uptake of mentioned tools such as uv, yet the evidence indicates this guidance often did not shorten the path to relevant files and may have added redundant steps.
    contradiction_note: This mechanism remains bounded because one ablation found LLM-generated context files helped when other documentation was removed, indicating the same behavioral steering can help in documentation-sparse settings.

    claim_text: Across the reported evaluations, supplying repository-level context files tended to lower coding-agent task success relative to giving no repository context.
    claim_kind: empirical_claim
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-01
    mechanism_or_bounds: The evidence establishes the direction of the aggregate performance effect, but it does not justify a single causal mechanism for why context files hurt success across all agents and tasks.
    contradiction_note: This overall result is in visible tension with the subset result that developer-provided context files improved performance relative to omitting context files.
    confidence: medium
  - claim_id: claim-002
    claim_text: Developer-provided context files performed better than LLM-generated context files on average, improving performance by 4% relative to no context while LLM-generated files reduced performance by 3%.
    claim_kind: comparative_claim
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-03
      - evidence-04
      - evidence-10
    mechanism_or_bounds: The comparison is supported across evaluated agents, but the evidence here does not isolate which properties of developer-written files caused the advantage.
    contradiction_note: This difference does not erase the broader negative aggregate finding, so the evidence supports a qualified comparison rather than a general claim that context files are beneficial.
    confidence: medium
  - claim_id: claim-003
    claim_text: Repository-level context files raised inference cost substantially, with reported average increases above 20%, and LLM-generated files also increased agent steps and reasoning-token use.
    claim_kind: bounded_claim
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-02
      - evidence-09
      - evidence-18

    contradiction_note: No direct contradiction is visible on the cost increase; the main tension is that higher cost did not consistently buy higher success.
    confidence: high
  - claim_id: claim-004

      - evidence-17
    mechanism_or_bounds: Context files appear to steer agents into additional searching, reading, testing, and tool invocation, including strong uptake of mentioned tools such as uv, yet the evidence indicates this guidance often did not shorten the path to relevant files and may have added redundant steps.
    contradiction_note: This mechanism remains bounded because one ablation found LLM-generated context files helped when other documentation was removed, indicating the same behavioral steering can help in documentation-sparse settings.
